speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six with Irving Eisner. Mr. Eisner, what did you do after liberation? Your father and you, what were your plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must tell you one thing, before we go ahead. See, I'm smiling most of the time, because I'm a happy man after all this, that I am able to tell you everything. What I did is the following. We came home. At that time, the Russians were in our place, because this part of Czechoslovakia, or Hungary, was joined to Russia. And the adults, like my father, knew what life is in Russia. Secondly, we had Jewish officers, high officers, in Chust. And they began, again, to make business with my father, again, in lumber. And they succeeded, both. My father made money, and they made money. And they told us, as good friends, [SPEAKING YIDDISH], which means friend Eisner, get away from here. Get away, because we are going to spread out. And don't live-- don't stay with us. It's not for you. Go far away. But you stay here, let's make business. And the day when they're going to close the border completely between Czechoslovakia and Russia, we will let you out. And they took us out the last day, and we went to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that happened-- I want to ask you-- the local people in Chust, who you knew before the war, what was their reaction to you upon your return?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, we didn't live too much in Chust. I went there to school, but my mother-- we lived in the village. We never went back to the village. In fact, my father didn't let me go back to the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Sojmy?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sojmy. He went, and he had a bad experience. There was one goy there, non-Jewish, a Catholic, that he was very friendly to us. And he worked with him. He took him in and told him-- but my father was afraid. He had the shivers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he didn't want to let me go. I only came to Chust. And at Chust, I didn't approach anybody. I didn't find anybody. I found a few friends who survived, people. I found my principal from the school. In fact, this principal wanted me to stay. He going to make for me a big shot, you know, in Russia, Communist. So we went to Chust, and we didn't find-- we didn't want to. My father decided, we don't stay on that soil where we lost our family. We are going away. And that's it. We don't want to stay. I didn't find anybody. Don't want to find anybody. So where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Czechoslovakia, and we were there til 1956. Yeah. 1940-- yeah, '46, after the war. Sorry. '46. Correct. And then we went-- our aim was to get away, because Czechoslovakia also became Communist. It wasn't the same as it was before. So we went to DP camps in Germany. It was called Waldstadt. And the village was called Poking. Waldstadt was a camp, Poking was a village. It had a number, if I remember, camp one thousand and seven-something. But it was called Waldstadt by the Americans. The Americans supplied us with everything-- food and-- everything was very, very, very fine over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with who?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there with my father and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Sarah, who lives in Israel. Three of us arrived to-- and in the house that we found in Chust before, like I told you, there were lots of ladies there, and men. And one the ladies, who became my second mother later on, she was also there. She lost her husband. And because I and my sister knew her very, very, very well from before the war, she never-- she couldn't give birth to children. It was her-- her husband's fault, as you call it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we suggested to our father to marry her. Plus, he has to go to come with us, to live with the kids. He was still young. So he married. We came to Germany. And there, in DP camp, he married that lady, who became my second mother. Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel [? Rosica. ?] She's now buried right beside my father in Haifa. She was, to me, like a mother, a mother can be. I wouldn't-- I would lie, if I would say some bad word. She was, to me, like a mother. And then, we were in DP camps. There were about 6,000 Jews in that camp. We had a good life there. And there, I found a girlfriend, who became my beloved wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Emma, or [? Simu. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name was [? Gottman. ?] She-- she was-- she survived in Budapest as a shiksa, as non-Jewish. And we married in Germany, not far from Landsberg, the camp of where we were in Waldstadt, Poking. And to me, it was, again, a bittersweet. I could say, in the land where I was tortured and put to death every day, I saw death, and death, I can celebrate my wedding. I can dance and sing. And I'm going to leave Germany. I will never, never, turn back to come here. And when we went, I like turn around and I threw a-- a piece of wood. And I said, I don't want to come back to see you. And I went back, nevertheless. But let me just go the story first. We married. And eight days after our wedding, we went to Israel. And my wife became pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that, when you went to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel, we went in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was already legal--

speaker: interviewee
text: Legal, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And after 11 months, my wife gave birth to my beautiful little son. I called him Nathan. Nathan means giving. First my zayde was called Nathan. But I also wrote in my book, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. God gave me life. My second one-- son was called Moshe Chaim-- also after relatives. One of the Moshe Chaim is a father of the second mother of mine. And I translate it. Moshe means [SPEAKING YIDDISH], to who I pull them out from the sorrows, from the troubles. And Chaim, Moshe Chaim, to give me life. And my little son was born in Canada. The first two was born-- were born in Israel. My little one, Murray, his name is Menachem. [SPEAKING YIDDISH], God has comforted me. Menachem means to comfort. So three sons-- two were born in Israel, one was born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remained in the DP camp for three years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First of all, I myself went to an-- to improve my English, I went to courses. And I mentioned before, I collected, I assembled children from the streets and started to teach them, because I made a promise once. And I taught kids who came from Russia, from who knows where. I took them together, I-- I played with them, and I taught them whatever I could. And we were very happy in those camps. Didn't have to worry about money, about food. We lived good there. And then we went to-- to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived in Israel as a survivor, what was the Israeli reaction to survivors coming to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, in generally. I don't know in generally, but I know they welcomed us very friendly. And we arrived with the [YIDDISH] with the boat to Haifa. They threw oranges to us, and they welcomed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were a new country. What could we expect from them? They were-- they were in trouble themselves. There was not too much food. You had to stay in line for-- for potatoes, and for eggs, and for sugar. But we took it with joy. Because we knew that's the way of survival. Thanks God to be alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave us a plain tent, one little tent with two iron beds. And not even pillows. Nothing. So that's how we started our life. And we didn't complain. We were young, and we believed we will-- we will make it somehow. Then my wife's mother, my mother-in-law, had some pillows she brought with. So she gave us-- I think it was pillow. And we lived happily. We lived happily in Israel. Then I-- I started working as a teacher. And we were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to qualify some kind of college in-- in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they needed so many-- so-- because I knew several languages. They wanted me to go to teach in a place where there are [YIDDISH], newcomers, from all kind of languages. And I knew about seven languages in that time. So they-- they send me to teach. Even adults I was teaching, from Yemen, how to tell the watch, how to put on shoes. You know, that's beside teaching-- regular stuff. And slowly, slowly, we-- we managed. It was very, very nice. We were happy in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, they gave us a shikkun-- a little apartment, you know? Well, we were young, you know? It was different. And the only reason we left, because my wife's parents. Her mother was a very sick woman-- kidney trouble. And they were in Canada. And she needed-- somebody must come and help her. So it was only who could help her was my wife. And we came here to help her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I continues life here. I started again teaching. And I was teaching here in Toronto, since 1953. And I'm still doing it. 1953, I started to teach here in Toronto-- two different schools. I started with Eitz Chaim for 10 years. And the rest, 30 years in the associated Hebrew schools. And the last few years, I am supervisor, or principal. And I'm still involved with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you are very happy person.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a very happy person.

speaker: interviewer
text: By temperament.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a very happy person.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I want to ask you a question, with your permission. How Is it possible to survive the Holocaust, and inspire children with hope, and not to despair?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very, very-- I-- I was asked this question different way, but your question is a hard question. But to me, I saw plenty of sorrows, plenty of sadnesses-- sadness. And I, to me, to be happy is more important than to be sad. What am I going to gain aggravating myself from the past? Am I going to gain something from it? From the past, I learn the present. And from the present, I'm learning what to do with the future. And I believe the only way to guarantee the survival of our people is through education. And I saw the loss of our people. And I want to see, and I'm seeing, the up-- the coming up, the growing up of our people. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. From sorrow, to joy. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. From sadness to happy days. And this is also why I took my job as a children-- to stay with children. Children are happy. Children are telling the truth, when they are little at least. Children are singing. Children are dancing. Children are cute. Even sometimes, you have some aggravation, but the simple, the joy, is more than aggravation. So by being with them, happy, I forget-- I forget the sorrows.

speaker: interviewer
text: What am I going to gain by being sad? What am I going to gain to have some claims, or anger against God? I know-- I know we suffered. You think he didn't suffer? I could quote you some sentences from the Bible.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH] It's written, I can't mention exactly where. Is a Chumash, I know. I'm with him in the sorrows. When Jewish are in trouble, God's in trouble too. So I have pity on Him too. He suffered together with us, even though he is almighty. But sometimes, nature, some things, you-- you can't. It [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Another sentence says, when He is in sorrows, He is with us, also he suffers. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] . I have pity on God too. He's suffering. I felt that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what are you going to gain? I said to God, let's drink l'chaim. Let's drink l'chaim. I know you make me suffer. I have pity on you, you have pity on me. But let's drink l'chaim. Let's be happy. And to aggravate yourself, to eat yourself, and to be sad, that's not how to-- how to survive and how to-- how to serve God. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. And I believe He's still the master of the world. I'm allowed to ask questions. And I have lots of questions. Do I get an answer? I think-- I think, if I am alive, and I have a beautiful family, that's my answer. I don't know how, what way. It's mystical. The Torah says-- it was Moshe Rabbeinu who was greater than I am. He was a prophet of the prophets. He said to God, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Show me. He was puzzled, Moshe Rabbeinu. There are people who are wicked, it is good for them. People who are-- he said to God, tell me, how do you manage this world? How do you charge people? He said to Moshe Rabbeinu, I'm sorry, you will never understand it. You will only see my face. You will not see-- you will see my back. Sorry. My back. You will not see the face. You will only know what happened You will never see what I'm going to do. You will never understand it. So if Moshe Rabbeinu didn't understand, who am I, Mr. Eisner? Who am I to understand it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH] We have two types of things in our life. [YIDDISH] means hidden. [YIDDISH] means open. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. The hidden things, which I don't understand, they belong to him. [YIDDISH], which is open, what I have to do. I-- I have to understand. And I understand I must build the future of our people through happiness. I will achieve much more. Like in school, you want to teach children through happiness, through goodness. You will achieve much more than through anger or through harsh-- hard-- hard way of approaching it. And that's why I choose a happy way. And I am a happy man, because I can tell this for everybody. And I am going to be happy, as long as I live, hopefully. But I'm happy after all these tragedies. Sure, life is-- is not exactly as you want it. But again, let's take it day by day and be happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another question I wanted to ask you. You came to Canada in 1953?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you've been teaching.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where have you taught in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only mainly in two schools, day schools, in Eitz Chaim, and in the associated schools. Those are the two major schools-- 10 years at Eitz Chaim, and 30 years in associated, and four-- and three years in Israel. So altogether, 43 years of my life for teaching. That's what I promised, and I'm still doing it. And I know, thanks God, I succeeded, pretty well. What I know from students and from parents, I did my job pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: When it came to being a parent yourself, what was it from your childhood that you were able to transmit to your children? And how did you do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I tried, and I'm still trying, to transfer to my children the values that my parents gave me. That was enough. I used to say to my kids when they were smaller, you know-- the three boys-- I wish that, when you grow up, you should give me and mom half of the respect which I gave to my father and my mother. [YIDDISH], they're giving me not half, they're giving me 100% respect. But in our society, in our time, I wished for half of it. And that is what I wanted them to continue-- respect for parents, respect for elders.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way, if any, could you tell for us-- tell us how your experiences have affected you as a parent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a Jewish parent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. I said this when I came back from the [YIDDISH], to be a better Jew, a better person, a stronger man. What I mean, stronger spiritually.

speaker: interviewer
text: There is for what to live. Don't be passive. Do something. Everybody of us, there is something we can do. We are living, you know, just for the sake of living. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] I told you in the beginning, if you have the [SPEAKING YIDDISH], I will not die. What you need is [YIDDISH]. If you live, you don't die. If you die, you don't live. Why do you have the double language? The [YIDDISH] is very, very, very plain. [SPEAKING YIDDISH], sure. But [SPEAKING YIDDISH], just to stay alive and to breathe the air is not enough. You know why, [YIDDISH]? Because the next word, [YIDDISH]. You live, yes, but you have to do something. [YIDDISH]. [YIDDISH] can mean lots of things. It could be to do something for your people. You can do something for the society, for-- for-- for mankind. Just to live is almost like [YIDDISH]. This is my way of life. So You've told your story in the Jewish day schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: At lots of schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I understand you've also told your story to a lot of public--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jews. Oh, yes. Almost as many non-Jewish as Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of response do you get from the non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they listen. They want to know. They want to know. They respect. They ask good questions. Usually, I'm speaking about 30 minutes in a class, without notes. Because every time I speak, something new comes up. And then I give them a chance to ask. And actually, they have tough questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had once a question from a public-- from a grade 13. I don't know til today, he is Jewish or not. He says to me-- he tells me, after all this, Mr. Eisner, you still wear a yarmulke. You still believe? So I told him, at least yarmulke is left. [LAUGHS] At least something is left. What else do I have? What else? What do I have? At least I believe and I live in it. You know, there's some tradition. What else? Do we have another choice? And then they ask me, do you hate Germans, for example, after what they have done? Again, I don't remember who asked this. I said, the fact is, I don't hate anybody. It could be German, or Jew, or Hungarian, or Ukrainian. I don't hate a human being. I don't hate. Why? Because I don't want human beings to suffer the pain which I have suffered. It hurts. It's painful. Why should a human being suffer?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I do hate a killer who wants to-- who's going to kill children or people, or a rapist, or who know-- a-- a murderer. And I don't care who he is. It can be a Jew. I don't care-- a German or Hungarian. But otherwise, I don't hate. There were cases in the camps when German ladies, they're going to work. I used to go in the back, always, a little short. They used to give me bread to eat. I don't-- I didn't have too much left, because if people saw the bread, they attacked me. And I was left a few crumbs in my palm. I had soldiers-- German soldiers from Romania-- it was occupied by the Germans-- who used to say to us, be smart. Take it easy. Don't work too hard. But if you see the Germans are coming with the dogs, start working. They were sitting underneath a bush, and we were watching. And we saw the Germans are coming. We started to say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And so they jumped up, those soldiers with the rifles, and they started to yell at us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people are people. But when people turn into animals-- and those German SS, and the Gestapo, they were trained for years to be void of any human feelings. And to them, a human being was nothing. Nothing. Those people, I hate. But otherwise, why should I hate people? Why should people suffer, like I suffered? Why? So I don't hate Germans, or anybody. I hate killers, robbers, murderers. Many of these children that you speak to, have-- have they heard about the Holocaust, what happened during the Second World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yep. They-- the Jewish mostly did, because they still have many grandparents or so, relatives. And many of us who is telling the stories, even though I know that the same proportion probably do not tell-- which I think, to my opinion, it's wrong. They should tell. But I don't want to go into this. But for example, I remember last speaking, what I did was, in Kipling. A whole class, grade 15, all black people. The whole class. I brought them a few slides from [YIDDISH], to show them the-- the-- the camps-- the crematorium, the gas-- the gas chambers, and so on, so on, so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were sitting, quiet. You could hear a needle fall on the floor. They were listening. The bell was ringing 4 o'clock to go home, they didn't want to move. The professor who, was sitting there, the teacher, they said to me, what is this? They never saw in their life boys and girls that age, the bell is ringing, they are sitting and listening to every word. They asked me questions. And then I asked a girl, did you learn about the Holocaust? And one girl stood up and said, Mr. Eisner, we never heard about it. We learned in our history, one sentence. During the war, there was a Holocaust. Not even mention Holocaust against Russians or against Jews. Nothing. So they don't know. They opened up-- they never heard about the crematoria, about gas chambers. Now, you just think about the far eastern countries. Do they know something about it? You take Japan, China. Do-- do they know something about the Holocaust? They don't know. So many people don't know. Among our Jewish people-- you know, I had-- I had a discussion. Some parents didn't want even to-- to hear about it. And we-- we had a big fight, teach it altogether. So we decided, from grade six and up, maybe, but it depends what language, you know, not to go too-- like my first book was written very soft language, you know? I could make this, you know, a little bit-- but you have to be careful. But to my opinion, every child, every Jewish child, should know about it. Has to know. How do you feel about people who deny the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would tell them very plain, come with me for a trip. Come with me for a trip. I will take you to Majdanek. I will take you to Birkenau, take you to Treblinka. You will see for yourself. What do you have-- what do you have to talk about it? Come with me, I'll be your guide.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Treblinka, while we were walking around, all of a sudden, they called me. We had a man with a video there, you know? A gentleman, a non-Jew, about my age, riding still a bike. He stands besides the gate, and he's talking to the photographer. They don't understand each other. So they knew I knew a little bit Polish, or Russian, or Ukrainian, you know, mixed together. Mr. Eisner, maybe you'll be  a translator. I said, OK, I'll try. So I say to him, [SPEAKING POLISH], which means, do you know what was here? He says to me, [SPEAKING POLISH]. They brought Jews, and they killed them. And he says to me-- says to me, see over there, the hills, my house. He can see every day what's happening. So he asked me, why do you want to know? So I told him. We-- I came from America, from Canada. And there are people in Canada who say that this is not true. It's all a-- a story. So he says to me, [SPEAKING POLISH] He says to me, tell them to come here to me. I will tell them what happened here. So I would tell those people, you don't believe it? Where is my mother? Where is my mother? Come with me, I'll show you where she was killed. So I can't deny it. To what do you attribute the success in getting people to follow the movements?

speaker: interviewee
text: Success of?

speaker: interviewer
text: That they are-- they are able to get people to follow them? How do you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- you always-- sorry. You always find people that you can-- you can talk to them, and they will-- they will listen to you. How did Hitler succeed? You keep on talking, and talking, and talking, until people start to say it is true. They start to believing. You take any human being-- start with children is even easier. You talk them in a story. They start to believe in it. That's how come they succeed. Their propaganda, their advertisements are succeeding. That's why I said at the beginning, it can happen again. Til recently, I saw in the paper a picture of a-- of-- of a neo-Nazi, a second Hitler. It was a picture with a swastika-- second Hitler. So you need people just to listen and listen. And then they start to believe in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister is alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: She survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where does she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lives in Israel, in Ramat Gan. She's very-- I told you I have that picture of one of her sons. Is one of the high, high officers in the Army. And he organized--

speaker: interviewer
text: In which army?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Israeli Army. And he organized that, every year, a group of-- [COUGHS] excuse me-- officers have to go to Birkenau to see the camps. He was the first who went there. And he gave me even a certificate what he wrote about it. Excuse me. [DRINKS WATER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So she is there. She is happy. Also, she has three children. And they're very happy. She's doing fine. Everything is fine. And I have a son now, who moved live in Israel, Ra'anana, as well. They're all happy. [COUGHS] Do you ever have any regrets about having left Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to say. We were happy in Israel too. But sometimes, you have conditions, you know? But we-- we were happy in Israel. If not this family sickness, I would have never left Israel. I didn't make as good as here, but we were happy. We were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Israel is-- you have to love the country. And we loved the country, especially after coming from the camps. What-- what better could it be? It was our home. To live in Israel, you have to love the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son will make it much better, money-wise, like he does in Israel, but he will never come back. He likes the country. And his wife, the same. They were both born in Israel, but as babies they came here.
